mnemonics.title: SCM_RIGHTS Steps
mnemonics.content: Remember as 'SEND C MESSAGE RIGHTS': Setup socket → Create msghdr → Allocate cmsg buffer → Fill cmsghdr (level=SOL_SOCKET, type=SCM_RIGHTS) → Put FD in cmsg_data → Send with sendmsg() → Receiver uses recvmsg() to get FD → Both close when done

mnemonics.title: FD_CLOEXEC meaning
mnemonics.content: CLOEXEC = 'CLose on EXECution' - automatically closes the file descriptor when a new program is executed via exec() system call.

acronyms.expansion: Socket Control Message - mechanism for passing auxiliary data like file descriptors
acronyms.usage: Used in sendmsg()/recvmsg() as ancillary data

acronyms.expansion: File Descriptor - integer handle for I/O operations
acronyms.usage: Used to reference open files, sockets, pipes in Unix/Linux

acronyms.expansion: Inter-Process Communication - methods for processes to communicate
acronyms.usage: File descriptor passing is a form of IPC

acronyms.expansion: Control Message - structure for passing control information
acronyms.usage: cmsghdr structure used for file descriptor transfer

acronyms.expansion: Address Family UNIX - socket domain for local IPC
acronyms.usage: Socket family used for passing file descriptors between local processes

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a 'hot potato' game where the sender holds an open file (represented as a key) and passes it through a Unix domain socket 'tunnel' to the receiver. Both now have keys to the same 'lock' (open file description). Closing your key doesn't affect the other's access.

Think of cmsghdr as an envelope: cmsg_len = total length, cmsg_level = 'who sent it' (SOL_SOCKET), cmsg_type = 'what's inside' (SCM_RIGHTS), and cmsg_data is the actual letter (file descriptor number).

Picture two separate desks (processes) with a drawer (kernel's open file table). Both desks have a drawer handle (FD) pointing to the SAME drawer. Moving one handle doesn't move the other, but they open the same drawer.

analogies.analogy: Like giving someone the keys to your car - you still have your keys, but now both of you can drive the same car. You both have independent keys (FDs) but access the same vehicle (open file). Closing your FD is like losing your keys - the other person can still drive.

Like sending a package with a special 'fragile' label. The regular message is the package body, but the 'fragile' label (SCM_RIGHTS) tells the carrier there's something extra (file descriptor) inside that needs special handling.
Like an automatic door closer that shuts when someone new enters (exec). If you set this flag, the door (FD) closes automatically when a new person (program) enters your room (process).
